Title: The potato R locus codes for dihydroflavonol 4-reductase

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: diploid W5281.2 (red allele donor); co-segregating red allele associated with R
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Prince Hairy (transformation recipient); Chieftain (cross parent); F1 progeny from Prince Hairy × Chieftain
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: doubled CaMV 35S promoter (constitutive) with tobacco etch virus translational enhancer
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Leucoanthocyanidins derived from DHK, DHQ, and DHM
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces dihydroflavonols to corresponding leucoanthocyanidins en route to anthocyanidin formation; the red allele supports reduction of DHK and also DHQ/DHM
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Pelargonidin-based anthocyanins; also peonidin- and purple anthocyanins depending on genetic background
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pigmentation of potato tuber skin and flowers
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: The red allele of dfr fully complements the potato R locus, enabling red or purple anthocyanin accumulation and red/purple pigmentation; in D- rrrr backgrounds it restores red/purple tuber color and reproduces native anthocyanin composition
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Constitutively expressed from CaMV 35S in transgenic lines; endogenous activity inferred in pigmented flowers/tuber skin depending on genetic background
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Greenhouse growth under 14 h photoperiod; 24-28°C day and 16-18°C night
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression in Prince Hairy; genetic crossing with Chieftain; PCR genotyping for D, R, and transgene; tuber/flower pigmentation phenotyping; HPLC profiling; LC-MS/MS anthocyanin identification
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The potato red-color R locus was shown to encode dihydroflavonol 4-reductase (dfr) by transgenic complementation. 2. Expression of the red dfr allele converted Prince Hairy flowers from pale blue to purple, indicating added red pigment production in a P-containing background. 3. In F1 progeny carrying D and the dfr transgene but lacking native R, all tubers were red or purple and never white, demonstrating complementation of R. 4. HPLC and LC-MS/MS showed that anthocyanin composition of dfr-transgenic red tubers matched that of the red-skinned cultivar Chieftain. 5. The red dfr allele appears able to utilize DHK, DHQ, and DHM, explaining production of pelargonidin-, peonidin-, and purple anthocyanin derivatives.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No obvious silencing after sexual hybridization was observed; no yield or growth penalties reported
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for engineering or breeding red-skinned potato in genotypes containing functional D and lacking dominant P activity; can be deployed transgenically or potentially by allele selection/editing to confer DHK-utilizing DFR activity
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper emphasizes that only the red allele supports pelargonidin-based red pigmentation, whereas non-red alleles still appear catalytically active for purple anthocyanin formation. Native anthocyanins identified in red tubers included pelargonidin-3-coumaryl-rutinoside-5-glucoside, pelargonidin-3-feruloyl-rutinoside-5-glucoside, pelargonidin-3-rutinoside-5-glucoside, plus minor peonidin derivatives.
